title: Room-in-roof insulation explained
description: Room-in-roof insulation traps heat in a loft conversion or attic bedroom. What it costs, what it saves, and how grants can cover it for UK homes.
blurb: What room-in-roof insulation is, what it costs, what it saves, and how to get it grant-funded.
lede: A loft conversion or an attic bedroom can be the coldest space in the house. The sloping ceilings, the little knee walls and the cupboards tucked behind them sit right under the roof, with very little stopping heat escaping straight out through the tiles. Room-in-roof insulation is the fix for exactly this kind of space, and unlike ordinary loft insulation it has to wrap around an awkward shape rather than just lie flat on a floor. This guide covers what the work involves, what it costs, roughly what it saves on a typical gas-heated home, and how the current grant schemes can cover most or all of the bill if you qualify.
short_answer: Room-in-roof insulation insulates the sloping ceilings, knee walls and small storage areas of a converted or habitable loft, rather than a flat floor. Paid for privately it usually runs from about £1,500 to £3,000 or more depending on the room, and on a gas-heated home it can trim somewhere around £200 to £400 a year off heating costs. If your home has a poor EPC and you are on a qualifying income, grant schemes such as ECO4 may fund it in full, so check the official eligibility checker first.

sections.html:
Ordinary loft insulation sits flat across the floor of a cold, unused loft. It keeps the heat in the rooms below and lets the loft itself go cold. That works fine until you turn the loft into living space. Once there is a bedroom, office or playroom up there, the cold-loft approach no longer applies, because you want the room itself to stay warm.

Room-in-roof insulation deals with the warm-loft situation. Instead of one flat layer, it follows the shape of the converted space and tackles several surfaces:

  • Sloping ceilings: insulation fitted between and under the rafters that follow the roof line.
  • Knee walls: the short vertical walls where the sloping ceiling meets the floor, usually with cold, uninsulated loft space hidden behind them.
  • Storage cupboards and eaves: the triangular voids behind the knee walls, often used as cupboards, which leak heat if left bare.
  • Flat ceiling sections and gable walls where they form part of the room.

The usual method is rigid insulation board cut to fit between the rafters and studs, often with a second layer fixed underneath and then plasterboarded over. Done properly it also keeps a ventilation gap above the insulation so the roof timbers stay dry. This is more involved than rolling out loft wool, which is why it costs more and is normally a job for a professional installer rather than a weekend DIY task.

sections.h2: What it costs and what it saves
sections.html:
Paid for privately, room-in-roof insulation typically costs somewhere between £1,500 and £3,000, and more for a large or complicated conversion. The price depends on the floor area, how many knee walls and eaves cupboards need doing, and whether the existing finish has to be stripped back and re-plastered.

The saving depends heavily on how bad the starting point is. A loft room that was converted years ago with little or no insulation loses a lot of heat, so insulating it well can make a real difference to both comfort and bills. The table below shows rough annual heating savings for a gas-heated home, based on the current price cap gas rate. Treat these as ballpark figures, because the real number depends on your roof, your heating habits and how cold the room was to begin with.

Starting pointRough heat saved per yearApprox. annual gas saving
Loft room with no insulation3,000 to 4,500 kWh£220 to £330
Loft room with thin or patchy insulation1,500 to 2,500 kWh£110 to £185
Already reasonably insulatedUnder 1,000 kWhUnder £75

At 7.33p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Savings are indicative and vary by property.

Paid for at full price, the work might take roughly five to twelve years to pay back through bill savings alone, so the comfort gain matters as much as the cash. If a grant covers the cost, the payback is immediate. A loft room that holds its heat also lets you turn down the rest of the house rather than chasing warmth from a single cold corner, which feeds into your wider heating running costs.

sections.h2: How it differs from a standard loft job
sections.html:
People often confuse the two because both involve the roof space. The simplest way to tell which you need is to ask whether the loft is used as a room.

  • Cold, unused loft: you want flat loft insulation on the floor. The guide on how thick loft insulation should be sets out the 270mm target.
  • Converted or habitable loft: you want room-in-roof insulation that follows the slopes and knee walls.

Room-in-roof work is fiddlier because it has to keep a ventilation path for the roof timbers, manage condensation risk, and tie in neatly with the existing finish. It also pairs naturally with draught-proofing, since loft rooms tend to have gaps around eaves hatches, around the stair opening and behind built-in cupboards. Sealing those gaps stops warm air sneaking out before the insulation even gets a chance to work. If the conversion has knee-wall cupboards on an outside wall, it is also worth checking whether cavity wall insulation applies to the gable, as the two measures often get assessed together.

sections.h2: Grants that can pay for it
sections.html:
Room-in-roof insulation is one of the measures the main government-backed schemes will fund, which makes it one of the better-value upgrades if you qualify. Two routes matter most.

ECO4

The Energy Company Obligation (ECO4) puts a duty on larger energy suppliers to fund efficiency measures in lower-income homes. It tends to target properties with a poor EPC rating (broadly bands D to G) where someone in the household is on a qualifying income or receives certain benefits. Where it applies, ECO4 can fund room-in-roof insulation in full, often as part of a package of measures decided by a retrofit assessment of the whole home. See the ECO4 scheme guide for the detail.

Great British Insulation Scheme

The Great British Insulation Scheme is broader and less tightly tied to income. It generally aims at homes in lower council tax bands with an EPC of D or below, and it usually funds one insulation measure per property. Under this scheme you may get the work free or pay a contribution, which you are told before any work goes ahead.

Both schemes start the same way: an online eligibility check, then a home survey if you qualify. Grant rules, income thresholds and the list of eligible measures change regularly, so check the current rules and amounts on the official GOV.UK and Ofgem pages before relying on any figure here. There are also wider grant routes and the Boiler Upgrade Scheme if you are weighing up several home upgrades at once.

Whether you pay or get it funded, a few things separate a good job from one that causes problems later:

  • Ventilation: the roof timbers need an air gap above the insulation. Pack insulation tight against the underside of the tiles and you risk trapped moisture and rot. A competent installer leaves the gap.
  • Continuity: the insulation should wrap behind knee walls and into the eaves, not stop at the visible ceiling. Cold bridges at the junctions undo a lot of the benefit.
  • Eaves cupboards: insulate the cupboard walls and fit a draught seal on the access doors, or those triangular voids stay cold and pull heat out of the room.
  • Condensation control: warmer surfaces help, but pair the work with sensible ventilation so moisture does not build up. The guide on condensation and mould covers the cheap fixes.

Use an installer registered with a recognised scheme such as TrustMark, particularly if any grant is involved, because grant funding almost always requires accredited installers and a proper retrofit assessment.

If you have a loft conversion or attic bedroom that feels cold and expensive to heat, room-in-roof insulation is usually the single most worthwhile upgrade for that space. At full price it costs roughly £1,500 to £3,000 and can save somewhere around £200 to £400 a year on a gas-heated home that started with little insulation, so the comfort gain often justifies it even before the bill saving. The real value, though, is in the grants: ECO4 and the Great British Insulation Scheme can cover much or all of the cost for qualifying homes, which turns a slow payback into an immediate win. Run the official eligibility checker first, get a proper survey, and make sure the installer leaves the roof ventilation gap and wraps the knee walls and eaves. Pair it with draught-proofing and sensible thermostat habits and the once-coldest room in the house can become one of the easiest to keep warm.

faq.q: Is room-in-roof insulation the same as loft insulation?
faq.a: No. Standard loft insulation is a flat layer on the floor of a cold, unused loft. Room-in-roof insulation follows the shape of a converted or habitable loft, insulating the sloping ceilings, the short knee walls and the storage areas behind them so the room itself stays warm.

faq.q: Can I get room-in-roof insulation for free?
faq.a: Possibly. Schemes such as ECO4 and the Great British Insulation Scheme can fund it in full or in part for homes with a poor EPC rating and, for ECO4, a qualifying household income or benefits. Use the official GOV.UK eligibility checker, as the rules and amounts change regularly.

faq.q: How much does room-in-roof insulation cost?
faq.a: Paid for privately it usually costs around £1,500 to £3,000, and more for a large or complicated conversion. The price depends on the floor area, the number of knee walls and eaves cupboards, and whether existing plasterwork has to be stripped and replaced.

faq.q: Will it cause damp or condensation?
faq.a: Not if it is fitted correctly. The installer must leave a ventilation gap above the insulation so the roof timbers can breathe, and you should keep the room reasonably ventilated. Packing insulation tight against the tiles with no air gap is what risks trapped moisture and rot.

Some confident DIYers tackle knee walls and eaves cupboards, but the sloping ceilings need careful work to keep the roof ventilated and avoid condensation, and grant-funded jobs must use accredited installers. For most people a professional is the safer choice.
